title: How to Do Great Work
gist: Paul Graham distills the cross-field intersection of how great work gets done into a four-step recipe (pick a field with aptitude and deep interest, reach the frontier, notice gaps, explore them), arguing curiosity is the master heuristic. He develops reusable frameworks an operator/founder can apply: exponential compounding from consistency, 'staying upwind' instead of planning, building successive versions starting small, taking affordable risk, and protecting morale as the substrate of ambitious work.

themes.theme: Four-step recipe & choosing what to work on

insights.claim: Great work follows a four-step recipe applicable across every field: choose a field, learn enough to reach the frontier of knowledge, notice the gaps there, and explore the promising ones.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Gives a portable, field-agnostic process for allocating effort toward differentiated output rather than diffuse busyness.
insights.quote: Four steps: choose a field, learn enough to get to the frontier, notice gaps, explore promising ones.
insights.locator: Four steps: choose a field...

insights.claim: Work worth choosing must satisfy three qualities — natural aptitude, deep interest, and scope to do great work — but the third rarely binds because ambitious people are already too conservative about scope.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Reframes opportunity selection: the constraint is matching personal fit, not finding a 'big enough' market.
insights.quote:
it has to be something you have a
natural aptitude for, that you have a
deep interest in, and that
offers scope to do great work.
insights.locator: The work you choose needs to have three qualities

insights.claim: The only way to figure out what to work on is by working — guess if unsure, start, and tolerate guessing wrong, because most kinds of work can't be evaluated except by doing them.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Bias-to-action heuristic: treat exploration as iterative sampling rather than upfront analysis.
insights.quote:
The way to figure out what to work on is by working. If you're not
sure what to work on, guess. But pick something and get going.
insights.locator: The way to figure out what to work on

insights.claim: When uncertain, optimize for interestingness; a field that does not become increasingly interesting as you learn more is probably not for you.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: A simple monitoring signal for whether to persist in or exit a line of work.
insights.quote:
But a field should become increasingly interesting as you
learn more about it. If it doesn't, it's probably not for you.
insights.locator: When in doubt, optimize for interestingness

insights.claim: Originality in choosing which problems to work on matters even more than originality in solving them, and is what distinguishes people who discover whole new fields.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Edge comes from contrarian problem choice, not just execution — the highest-leverage decision is what to pursue.
insights.quote:
Originality in choosing problems seems to matter even more than
originality in solving them.
insights.locator: Originality in choosing problems

insights.claim: Curiosity is the single best guide to great work because it selects the field, drives you to the frontier, makes you notice gaps, and propels exploration — it is the engine for all four steps.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Suggests routing attention and capital toward what one is genuinely, excessively curious about rather than what is fashionable.
insights.quote:
If you asked an oracle the
secret to doing great work and the oracle replied with a single
word, my bet would be on "curiosity."
insights.locator: Notice how often that word has come up

insights.claim: What you are excessively curious about — to a degree that would bore most other people — is the signal for what you should work on.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: A concrete filter for identifying personal edge: look for asymmetric, unusual interest.
insights.quote:
What are you excessively curious about — curious to a degree that
would bore most other people? That's what you're looking for.
insights.locator: What are you excessively curious about

insights.claim: Work that compounds yields exponential growth — learning makes further learning easier and audiences bring more audience — but the curve feels flat early, so people systematically underrate and under-invest in it.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Core investor mental model: undervalued compounding assets in their flat early phase are where outsized returns hide; push through the unrewarding phase deliberately.
insights.quote:
Something that grows exponentially can become so valuable that it's
worth making an extraordinary effort to get it started.
insights.locator: If you do work that compounds

insights.claim: Consistency dominates intensity: writing a page a day produces a book a year, so the people who do great things get something done daily rather than nothing, exploiting the cumulative effect we tend to underestimate.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Process discipline beats sporadic heroics; a model for evaluating durable execution in people and companies.
insights.quote:
People who do great things don't get a lot done every
day. They get something done, rather than nothing.
insights.locator: Writing a page a day

insights.claim: For most ambitious work the right strategy is not to plan but to 'stay upwind' — at each stage do whatever is most interesting and preserves the best future options — because planning only works for achievements describable in advance.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Optionality-preserving decision rule for navigating uncertainty where the prize can't be specified upfront.
insights.quote:
At each stage do whatever seems most
interesting and gives you the best options for the future. I call
this approach "staying upwind."
insights.locator: I think for most people who want to do great work

insights.claim: Planning is a necessary evil — a response to inflexible media or the need to coordinate many people — and if you keep projects small and use flexible media you can let designs evolve instead.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Lower coordination cost and flexible tooling reduce the need for upfront planning, favoring iterative product development.
insights.quote:
Planning per se isn't good. It's sometimes necessary, but it's a
necessary evil — a response to unforgiving conditions.
insights.locator: Planning per se isn't good

insights.claim: Make what you yourself want — write the story you want to read, build the tool you want to use — because trying to make what an imaginary more-sophisticated audience wants leads you astray.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Founder heuristic for product fit: scratch your own itch; your friends become the initial audience.
insights.quote:
Write the story you want to read; build the tool
you want to use.
insights.locator: If you're making something for people

themes.theme: Successive versions: start small, ship, evolve

insights.claim: Great things are almost always made in successive versions — start with something small, get it in front of people quickly, and evolve it, so the final version is cleverer and more ambitious than anything you could have planned.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Validates the MVP / iterate model over big upfront design; a lens for judging product development teams.
insights.quote:
Great things are almost always made in
successive versions.
insights.locator: How do you get from starting small

insights.claim: An early version dismissed by people as a 'toy' is a good sign — it means the project has everything a new idea needs except scale, which tends to follow.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Maps to the disruptive-innovation pattern: dismissed 'toys' are early-stage opportunities incumbents and investors discount.
insights.quote:
An early version of a new project will sometimes be dismissed as a
toy. It's a good sign when people do this.
insights.locator: An early version of a new project

insights.claim: Begin by trying the simplest thing that could possibly work — surprisingly often it does, and if not, it at least gets you started.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Reduces activation cost and accelerates learning cycles in building products.
insights.quote:
Begin by trying the simplest thing that could possibly work.
Surprisingly often, it does.
insights.locator: Begin by trying the simplest thing

insights.claim: Big things start small: the initial versions of big things were often just experiments, side projects, or talks, so it pays to be promiscuously curious and start lots of small things.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Portfolio approach to innovation — seed many cheap bets because winners are unpredictable at the outset.
insights.quote:
Big things start small. The
initial versions of big things were often just experiments, or side
projects, or talks, which then grew into something bigger.
insights.locator: It's better to be promiscuously curious

insights.claim: Take as much risk as you can afford: in an efficient market risk is proportionate to reward, so seek high-expected-value bets rather than certainty, and if you're not failing occasionally you're being too conservative.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Explicit EV framing for risk-taking; absence of occasional failure is itself a warning sign of under-risking.
insights.quote:
In an efficient market, risk
is proportionate to reward, so don't look for certainty, but for a
bet with high expected value.
insights.locator: Take as much risk as you can afford

insights.claim: Being prolific is underrated — the more different things you try the greater the chance of a hit, but this necessarily means producing many bad ideas alongside the good ones.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Quantity of attempts drives discovery; tolerate a high failure rate as the cost of upside (Pauling: many ideas to have good ideas).
insights.quote:
You can't have a lot of good ideas without also
having a lot of bad ones.
insights.locator: Being prolific is underrated

insights.claim: Young/inexperienced people tend to under-take risk out of fear even though youth is precisely when one can afford the most risk; the advantages of youth are energy, time, optimism, and freedom, versus age's knowledge, efficiency, money, and power.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: A life-stage allocation framework; misperception of one's own risk capacity is a recurring error.
insights.quote:
The advantages of youth are energy,
time, optimism, and freedom. The advantages of age are knowledge,
efficiency, money, and power.
insights.locator: Use the advantages of youth

themes.theme: Finding new ideas: broken models, fashion & questions

insights.claim: New ideas require fixing a broken model of the world: ideas seem simultaneously novel and obvious because they're easy to see only after you do the hard work of noticing and repairing a model everyone shares.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Explains where non-consensus insight comes from — challenge the shared model others take for granted.
insights.quote:
When you fix a broken model, new ideas
become obvious.
insights.locator: It's that seeing the new idea usually requires

insights.claim: Every cherished but mistaken principle is surrounded by a 'dead zone' of valuable unexplored ideas; treating any field's quasi-religious attachments as suspect reveals what becomes possible if you discard them.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: A method for sourcing contrarian opportunities: identify the dogma in a field and explore what it forbids.
insights.quote:
Every cherished
but mistaken principle is surrounded by a
dead zone of valuable
ideas that are unexplored because they contradict it.
insights.locator: You could also discover overlooked ideas

insights.claim: A good new idea has to seem bad to most people, or someone would already have explored it; the target is ideas that seem crazy but the right kind of crazy — exciting and rich in implications rather than depressing.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Directly mirrors the venture thesis of seeking ideas that look wrong but are right; distinguishes good crazy from merely bad.
insights.quote:
a good new idea has to seem bad to
most people, or someone would have already explored it.
insights.locator: Indeed, if you think about it, a good new idea

insights.claim: Unfashionable problems are systematically undervalued because people respond to fashion as well as risk, so problems thought to be 'fully explored' or out of fashion are a rich, low-competition hunting ground.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Out-of-favor sectors/problems offer better odds because opportunists and critics are occupied elsewhere — a contrarian sourcing edge.
insights.quote: Unfashionable problems are undervalued.
insights.locator: Unfashionable problems are undervalued

insights.claim: The real insight in a big idea is often the question, not the answer; a really good question is a partial discovery, and great work often comes from returning to questions first noticed in youth.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Reframes research/edge as question-generation; carrying unanswered questions raises the odds of noticing a solution.
insights.quote:
People think big ideas are answers, but often the real insight was
in the question.
insights.locator: One of the biggest misconceptions about new ideas

insights.claim: Strictness combined with rule-breaking is what produces breakthroughs: only rule-breakers can be truly strict on questions that really matter, exemplified by Einstein seeing the implications of Maxwell's equations because he was stricter.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Identifies the rare temperament behind discovery — rigorous about reality yet willing to violate the prevailing model.
insights.quote:
in questions that really matter, only rule-breakers
can be truly strict.
insights.locator: Strictness plus rule-breaking

insights.claim: Per-project procrastination (deferring an ambitious project from year to year) is far more dangerous than per-day procrastination because it camouflages itself as industrious work on something else and sets off no alarms.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Counterintuitive risk: looking busy can mask years of avoiding the work that matters — periodically ask whether you're on the most important thing.
insights.quote: Per-project procrastination is far the more dangerous.
insights.locator: Since there are two senses of starting work

insights.claim: Work has an activation energy higher than the energy needed to continue, so it is legitimate to tell yourself proportionate small lies (e.g. 'I'll just read over what I've got') to cross the starting threshold.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Practical productivity mechanism for overcoming starting friction on hard tasks.
insights.quote:
Work has a sort of activation energy, both
per day and per project.
insights.locator: It will probably be harder to start working

insights.claim: Undirected thinking while walking, showering, or lying in bed solves problems frontal attack can't, but only works when interleaved with deliberate work that feeds it questions.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Argues for protecting mind-wandering time and avoiding distractions in the 'off' half of the work cycle, not just the on half.
insights.quote:
The
daydreaming has to be interleaved with deliberate work that feeds
it questions.
insights.locator: Work doesn't just happen when you're trying to

insights.claim: Earnestness — rooted in intellectual honesty — is a source of power, not just virtue, because seeing new ideas requires a sharp eye for truth that intellectual dishonesty destroys.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Frames honesty as an epistemic competitive advantage; any energy spent on seeming comes out of being good.
insights.quote:
To see new
ideas, you need an exceptionally sharp eye for the truth.
insights.locator: The core of being earnest

insights.claim: Aim to be the best, not merely good, because if you don't try to be the best you won't even be good — and there's an economy of scale that makes aiming highest strangely easier and more liberating.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Quality standard for evaluating both one's own work and operators: best-in-class ambition is qualitatively different from adequacy.
insights.quote:
if you don't try
to be the best, you won't even be good.
insights.locator: Consciously cultivate your taste

insights.claim: Express ideas in their most general form and they will be truer than you intended; the best ideas are tool-like, with implications across many different areas that others can build on.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: A test for durable, high-leverage ideas/products: generality and reusability predict broad downstream impact.
insights.quote:
If you express your ideas in the most general form, they'll be truer
than you intended.
insights.locator: Great work will often be tool-like

insights.claim: Build powerful tools to be gratuitously unrestrictive, because a powerful tool will by definition be used in unexpected ways, so err toward eliminating restrictions even when the benefit is unknown.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Platform-design principle: open, unrestrictive tools capture upside from unforeseen use cases.
insights.quote:
A powerful tool almost by definition
will be used in ways you didn't expect, so err on the side of
eliminating restrictions.
insights.locator: Similarly, if you're trying to build a powerful tool

insights.claim: Originality is the presence of new ideas, not the absence of old ones, so copying openly to learn is fine — the dangerous kind is unconscious copying where you're 'a train running on tracks laid down by someone else.'
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Reframes imitation: deliberate copying accelerates learning; only unexamined copying erodes differentiation.
insights.quote:
Originality is the
presence of new ideas, not the absence of old ones.
insights.locator: People new to a field will often copy

insights.claim: Copying an idea from one field into another is one of the most powerful moves, so deliberately learning about distant fields gives chance a hand by supplying fruitful analogies.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Cross-domain transfer is a repeatable innovation source — breadth of exposure increases the surface area for analogy-driven ideas.
insights.quote:
One of the most powerful kinds of copying is to copy something from
one field into another.
insights.locator: One of the most powerful kinds of copying

insights.claim: Quality of colleagues matters more than quantity, and great work demonstrably happens in clusters — one or two great colleagues who offer surprising insights can be the difference between doing great work and not.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Talent-density and geographic clustering are causal inputs to output quality — relevant to evaluating teams, ecosystems, and where to locate.
insights.quote:
the degree to which great work happens in clusters suggests
that one's colleagues often make the difference
insights.locator: Quality is more important than quantity

insights.claim: Some admired features of successful people are flaws they succeeded despite, and the easiest features to imitate are the most likely to be those flaws (e.g. being a jerk is not part of being talented).
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Warns against cargo-culting visible traits of high performers; separate causal drivers of success from incidental flaws.
insights.quote:
the features that are easiest to imitate are the
most likely to be the flaws.
insights.locator: Some of the features of things you admire

themes.theme: Morale as the substrate & ignoring gatekeepers

insights.claim: Morale is the basis of everything in ambitious work and compounds in both directions: high morale enables good work which raises morale, so when stuck it can be wise to switch to easier work just to get something done and reverse the cycle.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Treats morale as a managed asset with a feedback loop — a lever for sustaining performance through setbacks.
insights.quote:
Morale compounds via work: high morale helps you do good work, which
increases your morale and helps you do even better work.
insights.locator: Husband your morale

insights.claim: Doing great work is a depth-first search rooted in the desire to do it: the right rule is not 'never give up' but never let setbacks panic you into backtracking more than needed, and never abandon the root node.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: A precise model for persistence that permits tactical retreats while preserving the overarching goal.
insights.quote:
Doing great work is a depth-first search whose root node is the
desire to.
insights.locator: Doing great work is a depth-first search

insights.claim: Fame is the opinion of a large group and merely adds noise, whereas the opinion of people you respect is signal; the prestige of a type of work is at best a trailing indicator, so ask how well work could be done, not how prestigious it is.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Filter out prestige-chasing noise; prestige lags real value, so unprestigious-but-doable fields can be undervalued opportunities.
insights.quote:
The prestige of a type of work is at best a trailing indicator and
sometimes completely mistaken.
insights.locator: It's ok to want to impress other people

insights.claim: Don't think of yourself as dependent on a gatekeeper's 'big break' or take committee rejection to heart — the qualities that impress admissions officers and prize committees differ from those required to do great work, and most such decisions aren't part of a real feedback loop.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Discount institutional credentialing as a signal of capability; focus on the work and direct audience instead.
insights.quote:
The decisions of selection
committees are only meaningful to the extent that they're part of
a feedback loop, and very few are.
insights.locator: Don't think of yourself as dependent

insights.claim: A small, dedicated audience is sufficient to sustain you because the value of an audience does not grow anything like linearly with its size — good news for those just starting out.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Reframes early-stage traction: a handful of genuine fans validates and sustains, undercutting scale-obsessed metrics.
insights.quote:
If a handful of people
genuinely love what you're doing, that's enough.
insights.locator: An audience is a critical component of morale

insights.claim: Building something that lets people go around intermediaries and engage directly with their audience is probably a good idea, since escaping intermediaries is so liberating it can justify switching to an adjacent type of work.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: A durable startup thesis — disintermediation creates value; relevant for evaluating direct-to-audience/creator platforms.
insights.quote:
Building something that enables people to go around
intermediaries and engage directly with their audience is probably
a good idea.
insights.locator: Notes [28]

insights.claim: Schools train you to win by hacking the test, which is incompatible with great work because you 'can't trick God' — the way to beat the system is to focus on overlooked problems and solutions, not to skimp on the work.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Warns that optimizing for legible metrics (test-hacking) produces the opposite of real value — applies to any goodhart-prone system.
insights.quote:
But perhaps the worst thing schools do to you is train you to win
by hacking the test. You can't do great work by doing that. You
can't trick God.
insights.locator: But perhaps the worst thing schools do

insights.claim: The hardest part of inexperience is not what you don't know but 'what you do know that ain't so' — false beliefs from bad habits and schooling must be cleared before great work is possible.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Unlearning prior assumptions is a prerequisite for seeing clearly — relevant to why outsiders disrupt established players.
insights.quote: The other half is what you do know that ain't so.
insights.locator: But what you don't know is only half the problem

insights.claim: When learning something for the first time, pay attention to what seems wrong or missing and revisit it later; if a misgiving survives deeper knowledge, it probably represents an undiscovered idea — the fresh-eyes advantage of inexperience.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: A concrete method for harvesting newcomer perspective into discovery before assimilation dulls it.
insights.quote:
So when you're learning about something for the first time, pay
attention to things that seem wrong or missing.
insights.locator: So when you're learning about something

insights.claim: Novices and outsiders often make new discoveries because ignorance of a field's assumptions grants temporary 'passive independent-mindedness' — they break implicit rules without even knowing they exist.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Explains why incumbents are blindsided by entrants unencumbered by industry orthodoxy.
insights.quote:
This is why novices and outsiders often
make new discoveries; their ignorance of a field's assumptions acts
as a source of temporary passive independent-mindedness.
insights.locator: The other way to break rules
